title: 'Wonder Woman 1984' Is Here, on HBO Max. Plus: Paul Greengrass!
format: Wonder Woman 1984 lands at home on HBO Max for Christmas, day and date with a small theatrical run. Sean and Amanda dig into Patty Jenkins's sequel, the DC slate's continuity problems, and the streaming-vs-theatrical fight around it. Then Sean talks to Paul Greengrass about News of the World, his change-of-pace Western with Tom Hanks.

films.title: Wonder Woman 1984
films.note: Patty Jenkins's sequel, with Gal Gadot, Chris Pine, Kristen Wiig, and Pedro Pascal. Both hosts liked the first hour a lot and lose patience once the Dreamstone plot and the Cheetah showdown take over; Amanda says she'd have been happy if it ended at two hours. Jenkins's line about wanting a superhero movie where 'nobody dies, and she wins in the end with a conversation' gets some credit, even though the ending still turns on stopping a nuclear apocalypse. 155 minutes, released simultaneously in theaters and on HBO Max.

films.title: Wonder Woman
films.note: The original, also Jenkins/Gadot/Pine. Amanda's a big fan of it; both agree it mattered for putting a female superhero at the center of a blockbuster, chemistry between Gadot and Pine included.

films.title: Superman
films.note: Richard Donner's film comes up as the reference point for WW84's tone, especially the fireworks flying scene, which Sean says immediately recalls Lois Lane's first flight with Superman.

films.title: Aquaman
films.note: Cited as the only other DC movie with the same lighter, weirder energy Sean says sets the Wonder Woman films apart from the rest of the DCEU.

films.title: Commando
films.note: Amanda's comparison for the opening mall sequence, all the crime happening inside one shopping center.

films.title: Police Story
films.note: Sean's other reference point for the mall set piece, Jackie Chan's film.

films.title: Suicide Squad
films.note: David Ayer's film, brought up as the thing Warner Bros. is essentially redoing with James Gunn. Sean says the studio chopped up Ayer's original cut.

films.title: The Suicide Squad
films.note: James Gunn's version, arriving five years after the first, with some cast members returning (including Viola Davis) and others replaced. Both hosts find the near-identical titles genuinely confusing.

films.title: The Batman
films.note: Matt Reeves's next DC movie, mentioned in the upcoming slate. Sean is excited, cites the trailer, and says he's seen enough Batman movies to be genuinely looking forward to this one.

films.title: The Flash
films.note: Named in the 2022 DC slate as the Ezra Miller solo film.

films.title: Aquaman and the Lost Kingdom
films.note: Referred to on the show simply as 'Aquaman 2,' part of the upcoming slate Sean runs down.

films.title: Shazam! Fury of the Gods
films.note: Referred to on the show as 'Shazam 2,' also part of that slate.

films.title: Batman & Robin
films.note: Comes up as the prior example of DC's continuity whiplash: George Clooney playing Batman for one movie amid constant director and vision changes.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: Its $20 million opening weekend back in September is the box-office bar nothing else has cleared since, in the hosts' running tally of pandemic-era grosses.

films.title: The Croods: A New Age
films.note: Its $9.7 million Thanksgiving opening is the other data point, and Sean notes it topped Tenet in PVOD rentals over Christmas weekend.

films.title: Soul
films.note: Pixar's Christmas Day Disney+ release, competing with WW84 for family attention. Amanda cried watching it and calls it one of her favorites of the year, and they table a fuller discussion for the new year.

films.title: Promising Young Woman
films.note: Opening in theaters the same day; Carey Mulligan and Emerald Fennell had already been on the show to discuss it.

films.title: News of the World
films.note: The other theatrical release that day, which sets up the back half of the episode: Sean's interview with director Paul Greengrass.

films.title: News of the World
films.note: Greengrass's Western with Tom Hanks and Helena Zengel, adapted from Paulette Jiles's novel. He describes wanting a slower, more classical film after 22 July, and calls the mountain shootout sequence, shot after a two-hour rope climb to the location, the hardest logistical stretch of the shoot. Shot with cinematographer Dariusz Wolski, released by Universal day-and-date in theaters and (eventually) on demand.

films.title: The Searchers
films.note: The only film Greengrass rewatched while prepping, John Ford's Western, whose influence he says he felt on set.

films.title: Captain Phillips
films.note: His previous collaboration with Tom Hanks, brought up as the last time the two worked together.

films.title: Bloody Sunday
films.note: Greengrass's own dramatization of the 1972 Derry massacre. Sean's intro calls it 'Sunday, Bloody Sunday,' but the context (Greengrass's own filmography, alongside the Bourne films and Captain Phillips) makes clear this is the film meant, not John Schlesinger's 1971 film of that near-identical title.

films.title: 22 July
films.note: Greengrass's previous film, about the 2011 Norway terror attacks, which he says left him wanting to make something about 'the road to a better place' next.
